volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Comedy of Asses (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.1.104b)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Hoc agite sultis , spectatores , nunciam ,
sections.translation: Attend now , Spectators , if you please , forthwith to this , and may this matter turn out fortunately for me and for yourselves , and for this company , and for our employers [*] , and for our managers [*] . Now , crier , do you at once make all the people give attentive ear .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: age nunc reside , cave modo ne gratiis .
sections.translation: Come , be seated now , only be careful that ’ tis not for nought . Now I will tell you why I have come forward here , and what my intention is , that you may know the name of this play . For , so far as relates to the plot , it really is a short one . Now I will tell you what I said I was wishful to inform you upon .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: dicam : húic nomen graece Onagost fabulae ;
sections.translation: The name of this play in Greek is Onagos [*] — Demophilus [*] composed it — Marcus [*] Plautus turned it into Latin . He wishes it to be called Asinaria [*] if by your leaves it may be so . In this play there is both pleasantry and fun . ’ Tis a droll story ; kindly lend me your attention ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: ut vos , ut alias , pariter nunc Mars adiuvet .
sections.translation: may Mars , too , as , full oft at other times he has done , so give you now his aid .

sections.label: Section 1.1.16
sections.original: Sicut tuom vis unicum gnatum tuae
sections.translation: As you desire your own only son to survive your own existence , prosperous , and living on ; so by your lengthened years , and by that wife of yours of whom you stand in awe ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.original: si quid med erga hodie falsum dixeris ,
sections.translation: do I conjure you , that if this day you have said anything that ’ s false against myself , your wife may then survive your own existence , and that , she living , you , still alive , may come to utter destruction [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.23
sections.original: Per Dium Fidium quaeris : iurato mihi
sections.translation: By the Gods above [*] as to what you seek to know , I see that I must , perforce , speak out , whatever you question me upon , being thus conjured ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: ita me obstinate , ut non audeam
sections.translation: so determinedly have you accosted me , that I really do not dare otherwise than to disclose everything to you making all these enquiries . Say then at once what it is that you desire so much to know ; as I myself shall know , so will I let you know .

sections.label: Section 1.1.29
sections.original: Dic obsecro hercle serio quod te rogem ,
sections.translation: Troth now , prithee answer me seriously what I ask you ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.original: cave míhi mendaci quicquam .
sections.translation: take care that you tell me no falsehood .

sections.label: Section 1.1.30b
sections.original: Quin tu ergo rogas ?
sections.translation: Why , then , don ’ t you ask ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.31
sections.original: Num me illuc ducis , ubi lapis lapidem terit ?
sections.translation: Will you , then , be sending me there , where stone grinds stone [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.32
sections.original: Quid istúc est ? aut ubi istúc est terrarum loci ?
sections.translation: What place is that ? Or where in the world is that place ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.33
sections.original: Vbi flent nequam homines , qui polentam pinsitant .
sections.translation: Where worthless men are weeping , who breakfast upon pearled barley [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.34
sections.original: apud fústitudinas , ferricrepinas insulas ,
sections.translation: Why , in the islands of club-land and rattle- chains [*] ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: ubi vivos homines mortui incursant boves .
sections.translation: where dead oxen attack living men .

sections.label: Section 1.1.36
sections.original: Modo pol percepi , Libane , quid istuc sit loci :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I now understand , Libanus , what place it is ; that perhaps you mean , where the pearled barley is prepared .

sections.label: Section 1.1.37b
sections.original: Ah ,
sections.translation: O dear : I ’ m not speaking of that , i ’ faith , nor do I wish to speak of it . Troth now , prithee , do spit out [*] the words that you have spoken .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: Fiat , geratur mos tibi .
sections.translation: Be it so ; you shall be indulged .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40b
sections.original: Age , age usque excrea .
sections.translation: Come , come — hawk away .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41
sections.original: Etiamne ?
sections.translation: What , still more ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.41b
sections.original: Age quaeso hercle usque ex penitis faucibus .
sections.translation: Troth now ; prithee , do go on — still more .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.original: etiam amplius .
sections.translation: Even more still .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.original: Nam quo usque ?
sections.translation: Why , how long ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42c
sections.original: Vsque ad mortem volo .
sections.translation: I want you , even to the death —

sections.label: Section 1.1.43
sections.original: Cave sís malam rem .
sections.translation: Take you care of a woful mishap , if you please .

sections.label: Section 1.1.43b
sections.original: Vxoris dico , non tuam .
sections.translation: Of your wife , I mean , not of yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.44_45
sections.original: Dono te ob istuc dictum , ut expers sis metu .
sections.translation: For that speech , I give you leave to be free from apprehension .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46
sections.original: Di tibi dent quaecumque optes .
sections.translation: May the Gods grant you whatever you desire .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46b
sections.original: Redde operam mihi .
sections.translation: In return , give me your attention . Why should I ask this of you ? Or why should I threaten you , because you have not made me acquainted with it ? Or why , in fine , should I censure my son

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: patres ut faciunt ceteri ?
sections.translation: as other fathers do ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.50b
sections.original: Quid istúc novi est ?
sections.translation: What new affair is this ? ( Aside .) I wonder much what it is , and I ’ m in dread what the upshot of it may be .

sections.label: Section 1.1.52
sections.original: Equidem scio iam , filius quod amet meus
sections.translation: In fact , I ’ m now aware that my son ’ s in love with that Courtesan Philenium , that lives close by . Is not this as I say , Libanus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.54b
sections.original: Rectam instas viam .
sections.translation: You are upon the right track ; such is the fact . But a dreadful malady has overtaken him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.56
sections.original: Quid morbi est ?
sections.translation: What is the malady ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.56b
sections.original: Quia non suppetunt dictis data .
sections.translation: Why , that his presents don ’ t equal his promises .

sections.label: Section 1.1.57
sections.original: Tune es adiutor nunc amanti filio ?
sections.translation: Are you , then , one who assists my son in his amours ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.58
sections.original: Sum vero , et alter noster est Leonida .
sections.translation: I really am , and our Leonida is another .

sections.label: Section 1.1.59
sections.original: Bene hercle facitis ét a me initis gratiam .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you do kindly , and you gain thanks from me . But this wife of mine , Libanus , don ’ t you know what sort of a person she is ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.61
sections.original: Tu primus sentis , nos tamen in pretio sumus .
sections.translation: You are the first to experience it , but we give a guess beforehand .

sections.label: Section 1.1.62
sections.original: Fateór eam esse importunam atque incommodam .
sections.translation: I confess that she is troublesome and not to be pleased .

sections.label: Section 1.1.63
sections.original: Posterius istuc dicis quam credo tibi .
sections.translation: You say that later than I believed you in it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.64
sections.original: Omnes parentes , Libane , liberis suis ,
sections.translation: All parents , Libanus , who listen to me , will show indulgence to their children ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.66
sections.original: quippe quí mage amico utantur gnato et benevolo .
sections.translation: inasmuch as they will find their sons more kindly disposed and more affectionate ; and that do I desire to do myself . I wish to be loved by mine ; I wish myself to be like my father , who , for my sake , himself in the disguise of a ship-master , carried off from a procurer a female with whom I was in love ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.71
sections.original: neque puduit eum id aetatis sycophantias
sections.translation: nor was he ashamed , at that time of life , to devise stratagems , and to purchase with good turns me , his son , for himself . These ways of my father have I resolved to imitate . For to-day my son Argyrippus has entreated me

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: uti sibi amanti facerem argenti copiam ;
sections.translation: to give him a supply of money for his amours ; and I very much wish in that to oblige my son . I wish to forward his amours ; I wish him to be fond of myself , his father . Although his mother keeps him strictly , and with a tight rein , as fathers have been in the habit of doing , all that I dismiss .

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: praesertim quom is me dignum quoi concrederet
sections.translation: Especially as he has deemed me deserving , for him to entrust it to ire , I ought to pay all due regard to his feelings . Inasmuch as he has applied to me , as it is right that a respectful son should do , I wish him to have some money for him to give to his mistress ⋯

sections.label: Section 1.1.84
sections.original: Cupis id quod cupere te nequiquam intellego .
sections.translation: You are desiring that which I find you are do siring to no purpose .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: dotalem servom Sauream uxor tua
sections.translation: Your wife brought her servant Saurea with her on her marriage [*] , who has more in his control than you have .

sections.label: Section 1.1.87
sections.original: Argentum accepi , dote imperium vendidi .
sections.translation: I received money with her , and for the portion I sold my authority . Now I ’ ll compress into a few words what I want of you ; my son is now in need of twenty silver minae :

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: face id ut paratum iam sit .
sections.translation: do you manage that it may be forthwith found for him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.90b
sections.original: Vnde gentium ?
sections.translation: From what place in the world ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.91
sections.original: Me defraudato .
sections.translation: Cheat me of them .

sections.label: Section 1.1.91b
sections.original: Maxumas nugas agis :
sections.translation: You are talking downright nonsense . You are bidding me take the clothes from off a naked man . I , cheat you ?— come now , fly you without wings , please . What , am I to cheat you who have nothing in your power for your own self ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: nisi quid tu porro uxorem defraudaveris ?
sections.translation: Unless you have first cheated your wife out of something .

sections.label: Section 1.1.96
sections.original: Qua me , qua uxorem , qua tu servom Sauream
sections.translation: Impose upon or rob myself in any way you can , my wife in any way , my servant Saurea in any way . I promise you that it shall not prove to your detriment , if you effect it to-day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.99
sections.original: Iubeas una opera me piscari in aere ,
sections.translation: On the same principle you might bid me to fish in the air ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: venari autem rete iaculo in medio mari .†
sections.translation: and to hunt with a javelin in the midst of the sea .

sections.label: Section 1.1.101
sections.original: Tibi optionem sumito Leonidam ,
sections.translation: Take Leonida as your coadjutor [*] ; devise some plan or other , think of some expedient : bring it about that my son this day gets some money to give his mistress .

sections.label: Section 1.1.104b
sections.original: Quid ais tu , Demaenete ?
sections.translation: What say you , Demaenetus ⋯
